section.title: Job Answers Again
section.sectionRationale: Job answers Eliphaz's second speech with his sharpest description yet of feeling personally assaulted by God, pierced, torn, and surrounded like a target for archers. Yet in the middle of this darkest language, he reaches for one of the book's brightest flashes of hope: a witness in heaven, an advocate on high, someone who will plead for him with God as a man pleads for his neighbor.
section.thesis: Job's grief deepens into a vivid sense of divine assault, yet even here he does not abandon hope entirely, reaching instead for the conviction that a witness in heaven already sees his innocence and will one day plead his case.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Job's renewed rebuke of his friends as miserable comforters; his vivid, almost violent description of feeling torn and targeted by God Himself; his sudden flash of hope in a witness and advocate already in heaven; his continued lament over mockers who surround him and his broken spirit; and his closing questions about where any hope could possibly remain if Sheol is his only home.

units.label: Miserable comforters are you all
units.note: I could also speak like you

verses.text: Then Job answered:
"I have heard many things like these; miserable comforters are you all.
Is there no end to your long-winded speeches? What provokes you to continue testifying?
I could also speak like you if you were in my place; I could heap up words against you and shake my head at you.
But I would encourage you with my mouth, and the consolation of my lips would bring relief.

units.label: God has torn me
units.note: He has set me up as His target

verses.text: "Even if I speak, my pain is not relieved, and if I hold back, how will it go away?
Surely He has now exhausted me; You have devastated all my family.
You have bound me, and it has become a witness; my frailty rises up and testifies against me.

His anger has torn me and opposed me; He gnashes His teeth at me. 
My adversary pierces me with His eyes.

They open their mouths against me and strike my cheeks with contempt; 
they join together against me.
God has delivered me to unjust men; He has thrown me to the clutches of the wicked.
I was at ease, but He shattered me; He seized me by the neck and crushed me. He has set me up as His target;
His archers surround me. He pierces my kidneys without mercy and spills my gall on the ground.
He breaks me with wound upon wound; He rushes me like a mighty warrior.
I have sewn sackcloth over my skin; I have buried my horn in the dust.
My face is red with weeping, and deep shadows ring my eyes;
yet my hands are free of violence and my prayer is pure.
"O earth, do not cover my {{dam|blood}}; may my cry for help never be laid to rest.
Even now my {{ed|witness}} is in heaven, and my {{sahed|advocate}} is on high.
My friends are my scoffers as my eyes pour out tears to God.
Oh, that a man might plead with God as he pleads with his neighbor!
For when only a few years are past I will go the way of no return.

units.label: My spirit is broken
units.note: the innocent are stirred against the godless

verses.text: "My spirit is broken; my days are extinguished; the grave awaits me.
Surely mockers surround me, and my eyes must gaze at their rebellion.
Give me, I pray, the pledge You demand. Who else will be my guarantor?
You have closed their minds to understanding; therefore You will not exalt them.
If a man denounces his friends for a price, the eyes of his children will fail.
He has made me a byword among the people, a man in whose face they spit.
My eyes have grown dim with grief, and my whole body is but a shadow.
The upright are appalled at this, and the innocent are stirred against the godless.
Yet a righteous one holds to his way, and the one with clean hands grows stronger.

units.label: Where then is my hope?
units.note: will we go down together into the dust

verses.text: "But come back and try again, all of you. For I will not find a wise man among you.
My days have passed; my plans are broken off, even the desires of my heart.
They have turned night into day, making light seem near in the face of darkness.
If I look for Sheol as my home, if I spread out my bed in darkness,
and say to corruption, 'You are my father,' and to the worm, 'My mother,' or 'My sister,'
where then is my {{tiqvah|hope}}? Who can see any hope for me?
Will it go down to the gates of Sheol? Will we go down together into the dust?"

dam.gloss: Blood, life. Job's cry, do not cover my blood, echoes the blood of Abel crying out from the ground in Genesis 4:10, a plea that his suffering not be silenced or forgotten but heard as a genuine cry for justice.

ed.gloss: A witness, one who testifies to the truth of a matter. Job's declaration that his witness is in heaven marks a striking turn: even while feeling personally torn apart by God, he trusts that heaven itself holds testimony to his innocence.

sahed.gloss: An advocate or witness, a rare term, possibly an Aramaic loanword, appearing only here in the Old Testament. Paired with ed in the same verse, it intensifies Job's flash of hope: not just one witness but an advocate actively representing his case on high.

tiqvah.gloss: Hope, expectation. Job's repeated, aching question, where then is my hope, closes this speech on a note of apparent despair, yet the very fact that he keeps asking the question, rather than abandoning it, shows hope has not entirely died even here.

basic.context: Job again rebukes his friends as miserable comforters before describing, in his most violent language yet, how he feels personally targeted and torn apart by God, like a man shot at by archers or seized by the throat. Yet right at this darkest point, Job reaches for a sudden, striking hope: a witness in heaven and an advocate on high, someone who already knows the truth of his case and might one day plead it as a man pleads for his neighbor. He returns to lament, surrounded by mockers, his spirit broken, and closes by asking where any hope could remain if Sheol, the grave, is truly his only future.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage models the coexistence of despair and hope within a single, honest prayer. Job does not resolve his anguish before reaching for hope; the hope simply appears, briefly, in the middle of his darkest language, and then the darkness returns. This pattern, hope interrupting grief without fully dispelling it, is itself an important lesson for readers who expect faith to move in a straight line from despair to certainty.
basic.originalLanguages: Job cries that the earth not cover his dam, blood, echoing Abel's blood crying from the ground, and declares that his ed, witness, and sahed, advocate, a rare word found nowhere else in the Old Testament, are already in heaven. His closing question, where then is my tiqvah, hope, leaves the ache open rather than resolved, though the very act of asking shows hope persisting even in apparent despair.
basic.typology: Job's cry for a witness and advocate in heaven who could plead his case anticipates the New Testament's doctrine of Christ's heavenly intercession for His people, and his plea that the earth not cover his blood anticipates the greater blood of Christ, which, unlike Abel's, speaks a better word, not for vengeance but for mercy.
basic.christ: Job longs for a witness in heaven and an advocate on high, a role the New Testament reveals is filled by Christ Himself, who ever lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him. Job's cry that his blood not be covered but heard finds its ultimate answer in the blood of Christ, which speaks a better word than the blood of Abel, not crying for vengeance but securing mercy for all who trust in Him.
basic.god: God is experienced here as simultaneously assailant and hoped-for advocate, a tension the chapter does not resolve. Job does not abandon belief that God's own heaven holds a true witness to his case, even while he describes God's own hand as the source of his most violent suffering, holding together an honesty about pain and a stubborn hope that refuses to let go of God entirely.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 4:10 records Abel's blood crying out from the ground; Hebrews 12:24 says the blood of Jesus speaks a better word than the blood of Abel; Hebrews 7:25 declares Christ always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him, the fulfillment of Job's longed-for advocate.
basic.oneStory: In his darkest language yet, Job reaches for the book's brightest hope so far, a witness and advocate already in heaven. That flash of hope, appearing in the middle of unresolved anguish, anticipates the New Testament's full revelation of Christ as the very advocate and intercessor Job could only long for.

context.literary: This speech intensifies the violent, personal language Job uses of God's dealings with him, moving from the archer and warrior imagery of chapter 16 to the sudden hope of 16:19-21, then back into lament through chapter 17, a pattern of hope interrupting but not resolving grief that will recur even more powerfully in chapter 19.
context.historical: The image of an advocate or witness pleading a case reflects ancient legal custom where a wronged party might seek someone to arbitrate or testify on their behalf, the same longing Job voiced for a mediator in 9:33, now sharpened into the conviction that such a witness already exists in heaven.
context.canonical: Job's cry, do not cover my blood, deliberately echoes Genesis 4:10's account of Abel's blood, and the writer of Hebrews will later draw an explicit contrast between the blood of Abel and the greater, better word spoken by the blood of Christ, showing this thread runs through the whole of Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that even in the depths of feeling personally assaulted by God, hope can flash forward unexpectedly, without needing to resolve every question first, modeling honest faith that holds despair and hope together.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Job's raw language of feeling torn apart by God alongside his sudden hope in a heavenly witness; it does not prescribe using violent language about God as a normal pattern of prayer, but it does model, as abiding truth, that genuine hope can coexist with genuine anguish rather than requiring the anguish to be resolved first.

debates.question: Who or what is the 'witness' and 'advocate' Job speaks of in 16:19?

views.label: God Himself, understood as both the one Job accuses and the one he trusts to vindicate him
views.case: Job elsewhere addresses God directly as both adversary and hoped-for judge; the witness in heaven may simply be his conviction that God, rightly understood, already knows his innocence.

views.label: A distinct heavenly figure or intercessor, anticipating a mediator apart from the accusing party
views.case: The parallel with 9:33's longing for a mediator, and later 19:25's living Redeemer, suggests Job may be groping toward the idea of a distinct advocate who could plead his case, a hope the New Testament will show fulfilled in Christ's own intercessory role.

debates.bearing: Either reading preserves the chapter's central hope, that Job's true innocence is known and will ultimately be vindicated, and the language anticipates, without yet fully specifying, the New Testament's revelation of Christ as mediator and advocate.

study.originalLanguages: Job's cry that the earth not cover his dam, blood, echoes Abel's blood crying from the ground in Genesis 4:10. His declared ed, witness, and rare sahed, advocate, found only here in the Hebrew Bible, together voice a hope for vindication even amid violent complaint, and his closing, unresolved question about his tiqvah, hope, leaves the ache open rather than settled.

typology.type: Job's cry that the earth not cover his blood
typology.fulfillment: Hebrews 12:24, the blood of Jesus speaking a better word than the blood of Abel, securing mercy rather than crying for vengeance.

typology.type: The witness and advocate Job trusts is already in heaven
typology.fulfillment: Hebrews 7:25, Christ who always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him.

study.christ: Job's sudden hope, my witness is in heaven, my advocate is on high, names a longing that the New Testament reveals is fully answered in Jesus Christ, who lives forever to intercede for His people before the Father. And Job's plea that his own blood not be covered but heard finds its ultimate resolution in the blood of Christ, which, unlike Abel's cry for justice against a murderer, speaks a better word, securing forgiveness and peace for all who trust in Him.
study.god: God appears here as both the source of Job's most violent suffering and, in the very same speech, the one whose heaven Job trusts holds a true witness to his innocence. This is not contradiction resolved but honest tension held together, an unflinching picture of a faith that keeps speaking to God even while accusing Him, refusing to let go of either the complaint or the hope.

crossReferences.note: Abel's blood cries out from the ground, the background image behind Job's plea that the earth not cover his own blood.
The blood of Jesus speaks a better word than the blood of Abel, answering Job's cry for his blood to be heard, not for vengeance but for mercy.
Christ always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him, fulfilling Job's hope for a witness and advocate in heaven.

study.oneStory: In the middle of his most violent language about feeling torn apart by God, Job reaches for the book's brightest hope so far: a witness and advocate already in heaven who knows the truth of his case. That longing, unresolved here, finds its full answer in Jesus Christ, the advocate who lives forever to intercede for His people and whose blood speaks a better word than any cry for vengeance.

sermon.title: Why Suffering?
sermon.why: Begg preaches directly on Job 16:7-17, the heart of Job's description of feeling personally assailed by God, addressing the raw honesty of this passage's language about suffering at God's own hand.

quiz.q: What sudden hope does Job voice in the middle of his darkest complaint (16:18-19)?

options.text: That his witness is already in heaven and his advocate is on high, even while he describes feeling personally torn apart by God.
options.note: This flash of hope appears immediately after Job's most violent language about God's assault against him, without waiting for his anguish to be resolved first.

options.text: That heaven itself already holds testimony to the truth of his innocence, even though his friends have become his scoffers.
options.note: A true summary of Job's hope, though less precise than his exact naming of a witness and advocate already present on high.

options.text: That he has finally discovered his hidden sin.
options.note: Job's hope here concerns vindication of his innocence through a heavenly witness, not the discovery of hidden guilt.

options.text: That his friends will finally admit he is right.
options.note: Job's hope here is directed toward heaven, not toward his friends, whom he calls scoffers in the very next verse.

quiz.q: What image does Job use to describe how he feels God has treated him in chapter 16?

options.text: Like a target for archers, seized by the throat and crushed by a mighty warrior.
options.note: Verses 12-14 use this vivid, violent imagery of assault to describe Job's experience of suffering at what he perceives as God's own hand.

options.text: Like an enemy whose anger tears and opposes him, gnashing its teeth against him.
options.note: A true, related image from earlier in the same chapter, though less vivid than the specific archer and warrior imagery of verses 12-14.

options.text: Like a physician carefully treating a wound.
options.note: Job's language describes wounds being inflicted, wound upon wound, rather than a physician's careful healing.

options.text: Like a shepherd gently guiding his flock.
options.note: This is the opposite of the violent, assaultive imagery Job actually uses in this chapter.

quiz.q: Why is the phrase 'do not cover my blood' (16:18) significant?

options.text: It echoes Abel's blood crying out from the ground in Genesis 4:10, showing Job wants his suffering to be heard and acknowledged, not silenced or forgotten.
options.note: This deliberate echo connects Job's cry to one of the earliest biblical images of an unheard, unavenged wrong crying out for acknowledgment.

options.text: It shows Job wants his cry for help to never be laid to rest or forgotten.
options.note: Correctly captures Job's basic wish, though less precise than identifying the specific echo of Abel's blood from Genesis 4:10.

options.text: It has no connection to any other part of Scripture.
options.note: The connection to Genesis 4:10's account of Abel's blood is a widely recognized biblical echo.

options.text: It refers to a literal wound Job wants treated by a physician.
options.note: The phrase is figurative, about his cry for justice being heard, not a request for medical treatment.

quiz.q: How does chapter 17 close, and what does that reveal about Job's state?

options.text: With an unresolved question, where then is my hope, showing that even in apparent despair Job has not entirely stopped asking or hoping.
options.note: The very act of continuing to ask about hope, rather than falling silent, suggests hope persists even in this dark closing passage.

options.text: With Job picturing Sheol as his likely home, yet still asking whether any hope could go down there with him.
options.note: A true description of the chapter's final images, though less precise than naming the unresolved question itself as the closing note.

options.text: With a full explanation from God about the reason for Job's suffering.
options.note: God does not speak until much later in the book, chapters 38 to 41; no explanation is given here.

options.text: With a confident declaration that his suffering is now over.
options.note: The chapter closes in continued lament and unresolved questioning, not confident resolution.

quiz.q: How does Job's longing for a witness and advocate in heaven connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates Christ's ongoing intercession for His people, since Hebrews 7:25 says He always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him.
options.note: This connection presents Job's hope as a genuine anticipation of the New Testament's fuller revelation of Christ's mediatorial and intercessory role.

options.text: It points toward a heavenly figure who represents believers before God, an idea the New Testament develops much further.
options.note: Correct in its general direction, though less precise than naming Hebrews 7:25's specific answer of Christ's continual intercession.

options.text: It refers only to Job's own future vindication in this life.
options.note: While Job is restored later in the book, this specific image of a witness and advocate in heaven points to something larger than earthly restoration, ultimately fulfilled in Christ's heavenly intercession.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to later biblical revelation.
options.note: The language of an advocate and witness in heaven closely anticipates Christ's intercessory role as described in Hebrews.

questions: Right in the middle of his darkest complaint, Job reaches for hope in a heavenly witness. Has hope ever surprised you in the middle of grief, before your circumstances had actually changed?

Job describes feeling personally targeted and torn apart, yet still trusts someone in heaven knows the truth of his case. Where do you need to trust that God, or someone, sees the truth of your situation even when it feels like no one around you does?
Job's cry, do not cover my blood, is a plea to be heard rather than silenced or forgotten. What pain in your own life have you tried to bury rather than bring honestly into the light?

Job longs for an advocate who could plead his case with God, a longing the New Testament says is answered in Christ's ongoing intercession. How does knowing Christ intercedes for you change how you pray about something you cannot resolve on your own?
This passage ends without full resolution, still asking where hope can be found. What helps you keep asking, rather than giving up entirely, when your own hope feels nearly exhausted?

translationNotes.note: Do not cover my blood deliberately echoes the language of Genesis 4:10, where Abel's blood cries out from the ground, casting Job's suffering as an unheard wrong crying out for acknowledgment and justice.

Advocate renders sahed, a rare term appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible, likely related to an Aramaic root for witness; paired with ed, witness, in the same verse, it intensifies Job's sudden hope of heavenly vindication.
Guarantor renders a legal term for someone who posts security or surety on another's behalf, continuing the book's recurring legal imagery of Job seeking someone to stand as his advocate before God.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 16:1-17:16. The em dash in verse 17:11 of the source text was replaced with a comma per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
